editorial.title: The koel war: why i’m considering serving an eviction notice to nature
editorial.teaser: Nature is majestic, they say. They neglected to mention that nature screams like a frantic toddler at 120 decibels from your windowsill at 3:00 am.
editorial.body:
There is a specific, creeping madness that descends upon the leafy suburbs of the northern beaches at this time of year. It isn’t the humidity, nor is it the existential dread of the upcoming holiday season.

it is the return of the asian koel—the feathered alarm clock from hell that, unfortunately for us all, lacks a snooze button. For the uninitiated—or the blessedly deaf—the koel does not "sing." it does not warble pleasantries like a magpie or laugh with the hearty, bogan gusto of a kookaburra.

no, the koel engages in advanced psychological warfare. Its call is a rising inflection of panic, a manic *“wooo-ooo”* that escalates in pitch and hysteria until it sounds remarkably like a smoke detector having a nervous breakdown.

we, the residents of the northern beaches, pride ourselves on our connection to the environment. We protest overdevelopment; we support bushfire readiness; we politely ignore the bin chickens.

but there is a limit to our ecological benevolence, and that limit is 3:14 am on a tuesday. One lies awake, staring at the ceiling, trapped in a suspense thriller.

the silence between calls is not peaceful; it is pregnant with dread. Is he done?

has he flown to a different suburb? no.

he is merely inhaling. *koo-el.

koo-el.* it is the sound of sleep deprivation manifesting as a physical entity. I propose we stop worrying about jetstar cancelling domestic flights and focus on cancelling this specific migration pattern.

we need a noise abatement order for the canopy. If a local pub played techno at this volume in a residential zone, the council would shut it down in minutes.

yet, because this acoustic vandal has wings and is technically "biodiversity," we are forced to endure nightly torture. I am not suggesting we harm them.

i am simply suggesting we train the local possums to direct the birds exclusively toward the properties of heavy snorers. It seems only fair.
editorial.author: Professor Remy
